title: The Prologue and Tale of Beryn: source lineage
summary: CME00044 is the repository XML representation of the two-part Middle English Prologue and Tale of Beryn, including the 1909 and 1887 title pages and edited pp. 1–120 of Furnivall and Stone's EETS volume. The 1909 issue reused plates from the Chaucer Society edition, whose title page is dated 1887 and which directly transcribed the unique witness now cataloged as Alnwick Castle, Collection of the Duke of Northumberland, MS 455; the edition also consulted John Urry's first printing of 1721. The work occupies ff. 180r–235r of the complete 287-leaf parchment textblock. No public complete digital facsimile of MS 455 was found. One LMES image of f. 180r is viewable for private study and deliberately remains uncached. A 2009 Senshu University printed facsimile represents the complete manuscript as a separate physical surrogate, while Alnwick offers onsite access and priced reproduction by arrangement. Any reproduction request should cover the entire present codex and all available physical surfaces, then map ff. 180r–235r only as the Beryn work portion.
review_notes: Current shelfmark MS 455 and the legacy forms MS 55 or Nthld 55 are modeled as designations of one witness, not as separate manuscripts.

A whole-object request means covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, two front and two rear paper flyleaves, all 287 parchment leaves including blanks and adjacent works, and edge views when offered. The Beryn work portion is then identified separately as ff. 180r–235r.
The 2009 Senshu facsimile is cataloged as a full-manuscript printed surrogate, but public records do not establish whether it reproduces every non-textblock surface.

The LMES image is a selected view of f. 180r, not a complete digital manuscript. It was not downloaded because LMES and holding-library terms limit manuscript-image use to private study absent written permission.
The British Library's official Alnwick microfilm list includes adjacent shelfmarks but omits MS 455; this is negative evidence only and does not prove that no microfilm exists anywhere.

The work-line total of 4,026 in the Furnivall/CME representation includes a two-line Latin colophon; Bowers prints a 4,022-line modern text. The differing editorial lineations are retained rather than silently reconciled.

The repository cache contains complete downloadable editions and complete access-policy documents, not a manuscript facsimile. Cache coverage labels apply to each downloaded source object, not to MS 455.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00044
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID are all CME00044.
TEMPHEAD records ‘8-22-16’; this ambiguous inherited notation is not normalized as an encoding date.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00044
entities.description: Public electronic text derived from selected matter in the 1909 EETS issue: its title page, the embedded 1887 Chaucer Society title page, and the edited poem on printed pp. 1–120. It omits the edition's foreword and introduction, Clouston's abstract, notes, glossary, maps, and other back matter.

entities.label: Furnivall and Stone, The Tale of Beryn (1909 EETS issue)
entities.description: EETS Extra Series 105 issue of the Chaucer Society text. Its pages were printed from casts of the earlier plates and retain the 1887 title page after the new EETS title page. Furnivall and Stone transcribed the unique Northumberland witness and compared it with Urry's 1721 print; Furnivall's bracketed interventions include supplied letters, words, phrases, and several conjectural lines.

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall and W. G. Stone, eds., The Tale of Beryn, with a Prologue of the Merry Adventure of the Pardoner with a Tapster at Canterbury, EETS Extra Series 105 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society, 1909).
bibliographic.title: The Tale of Beryn, with a Prologue of the Merry Adventure of the Pardoner with a Tapster at Canterbury
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 105
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

physical_description.extent: xii + 238 pages, with inserted pages 200a–200b and plates
notes: CME00044 represents the two title pages and poem on pp. 1–120, not the complete volume.

entities.label: Chaucer Society edition of The Tale of Beryn (1876–1887; title page 1887)
entities.description: The earlier plate issue from which the 1909 EETS text was reproduced. Cataloging describes a publication span of 1876–1887, while the title page embedded in the EETS volume is dated 1887. Furnivall's foreword is dated 13 August 1888, so publication and paratext dates are preserved without forcing a single undifferentiated date.

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall and W. G. Stone, eds., The Tale of Beryn, Chaucer Society, Second Series 17 and 24 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1876–1887; title page dated 1887).
bibliographic.title: The Tale of Beryn, with a Prologue of the Merry Adventure of the Pardoner with a Tapster at Canterbury
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Chaucer Society

entities.label: John Urry, The Works of Geoffrey Chaucer (1721)
entities.description: The first printed edition of the Beryn Prologue and Tale, prepared from the Northumberland manuscript then lent by the Hon. Mrs. Thynne. Furnivall and Stone consulted and collated Urry's text but also worked directly from the manuscript.

bibliographic.citation: John Urry, ed., The Works of Geoffrey Chaucer, Compared with the Former Editions, and Many Valuable MSS. (London: Bernard Lintot, 1721).
bibliographic.title: The Works of Geoffrey Chaucer, Compared with the Former Editions, and Many Valuable MSS.

entities.label: Alnwick Castle, Collection of the Duke of Northumberland, MS 455
entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of the Prologue and Tale of Beryn, which occupies ff. 180r–235r within a parchment Canterbury Tales codex. LMES dates the manuscript 1450–1475, attributes the Beryn portion to its Beryn Scribe, and localizes that scribe's language to LALME Linguistic Profile 6040 in Essex. This witness-level dialect evidence is not treated as proof that the work was composed in Essex.

holding.notes: No exact repository-owned public child catalog record for MS 455 was established. LMES provides the exact scholarly description; Alnwick's archive page provides repository access information.

physical_description.support: Parchment textblock with modern paper flyleaves
physical_description.extent: Two front paper flyleaves, 287 parchment textblock leaves, and two rear paper flyleaves
physical_description.format: Illuminated manuscript codex; quires principally of eight
physical_description.dimensions: Approximately 308 × 198 mm; written space approximately 220 × 100–110 mm
notes: LMES records red and blue paraphs and blue lombard initials with red penwork.
The Beryn work occupies ff. 180r–235r.
The exact historical relationship between legacy MS 55 and current MS 455 remains to be documented, but the sources use them for the same physical witness.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of the 1909 EETS Beryn volume
entities.description: A 282-page scan retaining the complete printed volume and copy-specific scan matter. CME00044's source selection is discontinuous: the 1909 title page, the 1887 title page, and the poem on printed pp. 1–120.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Urry's 1721 Works of Chaucer
entities.description: A complete 780-page PDF surrogate of Urry's edition. The Beryn Prologue and Tale appears at PDF pp. 660–692, corresponding to printed pp. 594–626.

entities.label: John M. Bowers, The Canterbury Tales: Fifteenth-Century Continuations and Additions (1992)
entities.description: A later comparison edition containing the Canterbury Interlude and Merchant's Tale of Beryn. It independently transcribes MS 455, adopts more conservative editorial treatment than Furnivall, and prints a 4,022-line Beryn section. It is not upstream of CME00044.

bibliographic.citation: John M. Bowers, ed., The Canterbury Tales: Fifteenth-Century Continuations and Additions (Kalamazoo, MI: Medieval Institute Publications, 1992).
bibliographic.title: The Canterbury Tales: Fifteenth-Century Continuations and Additions

entities.label: Senshu University printed facsimile of Alnwick MS 455 (2009)
entities.description: A large-format printed facsimile cataloged as reproducing the complete illuminated manuscript. It is a separate physical surrogate available through institutional library holdings, not a verified public digital download. Public catalog records do not specify whether every binding surface, pastedown, flyleaf, blank, or edge is reproduced.

date_statements.label: Publication
date_statements.value: 2009; British Library catalog also displays 2010 publication data

bibliographic.citation: Tomonori Matsushita, repr., The Canterbury Tales: Illuminated Manuscript on Vellum: Facsimile of Alnwick, Duke of Northumberland MS. 455 (Tokyo: Senshu University Center for Research on Language and Culture / Institute for Development of Social Intelligence, 2009).
bibliographic.title: The Canterbury Tales: Illuminated Manuscript on Vellum: Facsimile of Alnwick, Duke of Northumberland MS. 455
bibliographic.publisher: Senshu University Center for Research on Language and Culture / Institute for Development of Social Intelligence

entities.label: LMES selected image of Alnwick MS 455, f. 180r
entities.description: A single view-only full-page image of the work's opening leaf, exposed through the LMES handwriting-aspect interface. It covers only f. 180r and is not a complete manuscript facsimile.
entities.reproduction_format: Zoomify web image, selected leaf only
notes: Deliberately not cached: the displayed terms reserve manuscript-image copyright to holding libraries and require written permission beyond private study.

entities.label: Late Medieval English Scribes description of Alnwick MS 455
entities.description: Exact scholarly manuscript description supplying current shelfmark, date, support, dimensions, extent, quiring, decoration, Beryn Scribe attribution, LALME LP 6040, Essex localization, and the work's opening context.

entities.label: Alnwick Castle Archive access, imaging, and microfilm guidance
entities.description: Repository guidance documenting onsite research, charges, image-order limits and prices, supplied-image restrictions, publication permission, and the official British Library microfilm access list. The list omits MS 455 while listing neighboring shelfmarks.

access.access_method: Open the exact LMES manuscript record.
View the selected f. 180r image through the LMES aspect page. No local copy was made.
access.format: View-only Zoomify image of one leaf
notes: Coverage is selected image only: f. 180r, the work's opening leaf.
The underlying DHI asset URL returned HTTP 403 to direct automated access and was not bypassed.
LMES warns that its site is expected to go offline in July 2026; the dated access finding is retained.

access.access_method: Use the National Diet Library record and request the physical facsimile under call number YP51-B254.
Request the physical facsimile at the British Library under shelfmark MS Facsimile 2228; onsite consultation requires a Reader Pass and advance ordering.
Apply to the Archivist for an appointment. The 2024 guide describes Wednesday and Thursday access from 1 April through 23 October, subject to approval and conservation constraints.
access.format: Onsite consultation of the physical manuscript
notes: The 2024 guide quotes £55 per research day and £50 per day for a camera permit, including five captures; current charges should be confirmed before travel.

access.access_method: Request a quotation for complete-object photography. The standard in-house service is capped at 30 captures, so the entire codex requires a larger or external order by arrangement.
notes: The 2023 sheet quotes a £40 administration fee plus per-image charges of £3 per double-page spread, £5 per single image up to A4, £8 up to A1, and £14 above A1, with a possible £30 large-item processing fee. A complete codex would require a bespoke quotation.

Request the entire present manuscript object, not only the Beryn leaves: covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, both front paper flyleaves, all 287 parchment leaves including blanks and adjacent Canterbury Tales material, both rear paper flyleaves, and edges or other physical views when offered.
In the resulting complete delivery, associate CME00044 only with the work_portion ff. 180r–235r. Preserve all surrounding manuscript images as whole-object context.
Ask the archive to confirm current foliation, image sequence, color targets, resolution, file format, completeness, and whether any surfaces cannot be photographed.

Supplied images are restricted to noncommercial research and private study unless the archive gives separate written permission for reproduction, reuse, redistribution, publication, or Internet display.

access.access_method: No public complete digital facsimile or complete image inventory was verified. Use the repository enquiry route or consult the 2009 physical facsimile.
access.format: No public complete digital object found
notes: This is a dated negative finding, not a categorical claim that MS 455 has never been digitized.
LMES exposes only f. 180r; the Senshu surrogate is a physical printed volume; the official British Library microfilm access list omits MS 455.

access.access_method: Download the archive's current research guide, image-request terms, and historical microfilm access list.

rights.component: Underlying fifteenth-century Middle English and Latin textual content
rights.basis: The medieval text predates modern copyright; this does not extend to modern photographs, transcriptions, editorial matter, or contractual access terms.

rights.component: 1909 and reused nineteenth-century edited text, introduction, apparatus, and arrangement
rights.basis: The 1909 publication and its nineteenth-century plate source are in the U.S. public domain; scan derivatives and provider terms are separate.

rights.component: 1721 edited text, typography, arrangement, and historical edition content
rights.basis: Published in 1721, centuries before modern U.S. copyright terms.

rights.component: U-M electronic text and public item representation
rights.basis: U-M identifies the source materials as public domain and displays a No Copyright–United States/DPLA rights signal; institutional terms and non-U.S. rights remain separate.
The underlying volume is public domain in the United States, but the exact item metadata supplies no standardized license or rights URI for the scan derivative.
notes: Public download availability is not treated as a provider copyright dedication.

rights.component: Internet Archive PDF scan and item derivatives
rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide provider mark; legal effect varies by jurisdiction
rights.basis: The exact Internet Archive metadata associates the item with the Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0.

rights.component: Modern edited text, introduction, notes, and downloadable PDF
rights.jurisdiction: United States and other applicable jurisdictions
rights.basis: METS states that current digital editions are for individual use and directs reprint requests to its Executive Director; the separately supplied XML is licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.
notes: The cached PDF is retained for research comparison and should not be redistributed through Git without permission. The XML license does not automatically relicense the PDF or book design.

rights.component: Modern facsimile photography, selection, color reproduction, and book design
rights.jurisdiction: Japan, United Kingdom, and other applicable jurisdictions
rights.basis: The public library records establish bibliographic access but do not supply an open license or broad reproduction permission.
notes: Consultation or purchase of the printed facsimile does not imply permission to digitize or redistribute its images.

rights.component: Selected digital image of MS 455, f. 180r
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and other applicable jurisdictions
rights.basis: LMES states that manuscript images remain copyright of the holding libraries and limits use to private study absent written permission.
notes: The image remains uncached and must not be redistributed in the repository without written permission.

rights.component: Existing or commissioned photographs supplied by Alnwick Castle Archive
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and any jurisdiction specified in supplied terms
rights.basis: Alnwick's image-request terms limit supplied images to noncommercial research and private study and prohibit supplying them to others or reproducing, reusing, redistributing, publishing, or placing them on the Internet without prior written permission.
notes: A complete-object research order may be cached privately, but Git publication, print-on-demand use, or other commercial or public reuse requires explicit written permission covering that use.

editorial_practices.description: The electronic item includes the two edition title pages and poem on printed pp. 1–120, while excluding introductory and reference matter elsewhere in the complete volume.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviations, damaged or metrically deficient readings, and perceived omissions
editorial_practices.representation: Italic or bracketed editorial expansions and supplies, including several complete conjectural lines
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall explains that bracketed matter can range from expanded letters to supplied words, phrases, and lines. Formatting in CME00044 therefore cannot be interpreted as a single uniform manuscript phenomenon without comparison to MS 455.
The editors compared Urry's cut-out Beryn pages with the Northumberland manuscript and report proofreading the edition twice against the manuscript.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript punctuation, capitalization, abbreviations, word division, and letter forms
editorial_practices.representation: Modern punctuation and capitalization; expanded abbreviations; normalized word division, i/j and u/v; thorn rendered th; yogh normalized according to value
editorial_practices.description: Bowers's later edition supplies a useful conservative comparison but is itself an edited representation rather than a diplomatic facsimile.
editorial_practices.scope: Canterbury Interlude and Merchant's Tale of Beryn

evidence.citation: Dated audit of Alnwick, LMES, British Library, National Diet Library, Internet Archive, and general web discovery routes for a complete public digital facsimile of MS 455.
evidence.summary: No public complete digital codex or complete downloadable image inventory was found. Available surrogates were one LMES leaf, a physical printed facsimile, or repository-mediated access and imaging.
notes: No access control, terms gate, or human-verification mechanism was bypassed.

evidence.citation: Local checksum, size, format, and coverage audit of source-cache/CME00044 downloads.
evidence.summary: Verified the cached complete 1909, 1721, and 1992 PDFs; two Internet Archive metadata files; one Internet Archive page map; and three complete Alnwick access-policy documents against the exact recorded source URLs, byte sizes, and SHA-256 checksums.
notes: The cache is gitignored. LMES f. 180r and the physical Senshu facsimile are not cached.

open_questions.question: Who composed the two Beryn components, and were the Tale and Canterbury Interlude written at different dates?
next_steps: Retain anonymous responsibility and separate tentative component dates rather than assigning the work to Chaucer.
Evaluate Pearsall's c. 1410 proposal for the Tale, Brown's 1420 jubilee context for the Interlude, and arguments for a Canterbury or Christ Church connection from the cited scholarship.
Treat the Latin colophon's ‘son of the church of St Thomas’ as association evidence, not a personal name.

open_questions.question: Why do Furnivall, MED, and older scholarship call the witness MS 55 while current catalogs identify it as MS 455?
next_steps: Ask Alnwick Archive for shelfmark concordance or recataloging history.
Preserve MS 55 and Nthld 55 as legacy designations of MS 455 until documentary evidence explains the change.
Do not create a second witness entity from the number difference alone.

open_questions.question: Can a complete digital reproduction of the entire present MS 455 object be obtained, and exactly which physical surfaces does the 2009 printed facsimile reproduce?
next_steps: Request a bespoke Alnwick quotation for covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all four paper flyleaves, all 287 parchment leaves including blanks and adjacent works, and edges where offered.
Ask for an exact image inventory and identify ff. 180r–235r only as CME00044's work_portion within the full codex delivery.
Inspect the Senshu volume at NDL or BL MS Facsimile 2228 and record whether it includes bindings, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, color targets, and edges.
Obtain written terms separately for private cache retention, Git redistribution, scholarly publication, and commercial or print-on-demand reuse.

open_questions.question: Which specific Furnivall supplies and line divisions account for the 4,026 work lines represented in CME00044 versus Bowers's 4,022-line section?
next_steps: Align Furnivall/CME against Bowers and the f. 180r image without assuming that every italic or bracketed span represents the same manuscript feature.
Separate the 4,024 English lines and two Latin colophon lines from one apparatus quotation and two tail-note continuation lines included in the raw XML L count.
Use a complete manuscript facsimile, if obtained, to classify expansions, supplies, deletions, abbreviations, and layout evidence.
